epigraph.text: Test everything; hold fast what is good.

content.text:
We have seen that when lines are parallel, special angle relationships 
follow. Now we reverse the reasoning: if certain angle relationships 
hold, can we conclude the lines are parallel?

content.title: Converse of Corresponding Angles Postulate
content.content:
If two lines are cut by a transversal so that corresponding angles are 
congruent, then the lines are parallel.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Converse of Alternate Interior Angles Theorem
content.content:
If two lines are cut by a transversal so that alternate interior angles 
are congruent, then the lines are parallel.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Converse of Alternate Exterior Angles Theorem
content.content:
If two lines are cut by a transversal so that alternate exterior angles 
are congruent, then the lines are parallel.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Converse of Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem
content.content:
If two lines are cut by a transversal so that same-side interior angles 
are supplementary, then the lines are parallel.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let lines $\ell$ and $m$ be cut by transversal $t$, with $\angle 3 \cong \angle 6$ 
(alternate interior angles).

We want to prove $\ell \parallel m$.

Suppose, for contradiction, that $\ell \not\parallel m$. Then $\ell$ and 
$m$ intersect at some point $P$.

By the Exterior Angle Theorem (which we'll prove later), an exterior angle 
of a triangle is greater than either remote interior angle. This leads to 
a contradiction with our assumption that $\angle 3 \cong \angle 6$.

Therefore, $\ell \parallel m$.

content.title: Proving lines parallel
content.problem:
Given that $m\angle 1 = 75°$ and $m\angle 2 = 75°$ where $\angle 1$ and 
$\angle 2$ are corresponding angles, prove that $\ell \parallel m$.
content.solution:
$\angle 1 \cong \angle 2$ because $m\angle 1 = m\angle 2 = 75°$.

Since corresponding angles are congruent, by the Converse of the 
Corresponding Angles Postulate, $\ell \parallel m$.

content.title: Finding values for parallel lines
content.problem:
For what value of $x$ are lines $\ell$ and $m$ parallel if same-side 
interior angles measure $4x + 20$ and $6x - 10$?
content.solution:
For $\ell \parallel m$, same-side interior angles must be supplementary:
\[
 (4x + 20) + (6x - 10) = 180
\]
\[
 10x + 10 = 180
\]
\[
 x = 17
\]

When $x = 17$, the angles measure $88°$ and $92°$, which sum to $180°$.

marginNotes.text:
Now faith is the assurance of things hoped 
for, the conviction of things not seen.
